content.text: India is an agriculturally important country where two-thirds of its population is engaged in agricultural activities. Agriculture is a primary activity that produces most of the food we consume and provides raw materials for various industries (like textiles, sugar, etc.).
In addition to food and raw materials, some agricultural products like tea, coffee, and spices are also major export items, contributing to the country's economy.

This type of farming is practised on small patches of land with the help of primitive tools like hoe, dao, and digging sticks, and family/community labour. It depends upon monsoon, natural fertility of the soil, and suitability of other environmental conditions.

content.title: Slash and Burn Agriculture
content.text: It is a form of primitive subsistence farming where farmers clear a patch of land and produce cereals to sustain their family. When soil fertility decreases, they shift to a fresh patch of land, allowing nature to replenish the soil naturally.

content.title: Regional Names for Jhumming
items: Jhumming: North-eastern states (Assam, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Nagaland).
Pamlou: Manipur.
Dipa: Bastar (Chhattisgarh) and Andaman & Nicobar Islands.
Milpa: Mexico and Central America.
Roca: Brazil.
Ladang: Indonesia.

content.text: Practised in areas of high population pressure on land. It is labour-intensive farming where high doses of biochemical inputs and irrigation are used for obtaining higher production.

content.title: Pressure on Land
content.text: The 'right of inheritance' has led to the division of land among successive generations, making land-holding sizes uneconomical. Despite this, farmers continue to take maximum output from limited land due to the lack of alternative livelihoods.
The main characteristic is the use of higher doses of modern inputs like HYV (High Yielding Variety) seeds, chemical fertilisers, insecticides, and pesticides to obtain higher productivity.

content.title: Regional Variation
content.text: The degree of commercialisation varies. For example, rice is a commercial crop in Haryana and Punjab but a subsistence crop in Odisha.

content.title: Plantation Agriculture
items: It is a type of commercial farming where a single crop is grown on a large area.
It uses capital-intensive inputs and migrant labour.
Important plantation crops in India: Tea (Assam, North Bengal), Coffee (Karnataka), Rubber, Sugarcane, Banana.

rows: Paddy, Maize, Jowar, Bajra, Tur, Moong, Cotton, Jute

content.title: Green Revolution Impact
content.text: The success of the Green Revolution in Punjab, Haryana, and western Uttar Pradesh has been a major factor in the growth of rabi crops like wheat.

content.title: Conditions for Rice Growth
items: Type: Kharif crop.
Temperature: Above 25°C.
Humidity: High humidity with annual rainfall above 100 cm.
Producers: Plains of North and North-eastern India, coastal areas, and deltaic regions.
Note: India is the 2nd largest producer after China.

content.title: Conditions for Wheat Growth
items: Type: Rabi crop.
Climate: Cool growing season and bright sunshine at ripening.
Rainfall: 50 to 75 cm annual rainfall, evenly distributed.
Zones: Ganga-Satluj plains (NW) and black soil region of Deccan.
States: Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, Rajasthan.

content.text: Millets like Jowar, Bajra, and Ragi are known as coarse grains but have very high nutritional value. Ragi is rich in iron and calcium.
Maize is used as both food and fodder. It is a kharif crop (requires 21°C to 27°C) and grows well in old alluvial soil.
India is the largest producer and consumer of pulses. They are the major protein source in vegetarian diets. Except for Arhar, all pulses help restore soil fertility by fixing nitrogen.
Sugarcane is a tropical and subtropical crop (requires 21°C to 27°C, 75cm-100cm rainfall). India is the 2nd largest producer after Brazil.
Oil seeds cover 12% of the total cropped area. Main seeds: Groundnut (Kharif), Linseed, Mustard (Rabi), Sesamum. Gujarat is a leading groundnut producer.
Tea is a plantation crop introduced by the British. Requires warm, moist, frost-free climate and well-drained fertile soil. Major producers: Assam, Darjeeling hills.
Indian coffee (Arabica variety from Yemen) is known for its quality. Cultivation is confined to Baba Budan Hills, Nilgiri (Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu).
Rubber is an equatorial crop requiring rainfall above 200 cm and temperature above 25°C. Grown in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka.
Four major fibres: Cotton, Jute, Hemp, and Natural Silk. Rearing of silkworms for silk is called Sericulture.

content.title: Cotton and Jute
content.text: Cotton grows well in black soil of Deccan (requires 210 frost-free days). Jute, the 'Golden Fibre', grows in flood plains of West Bengal, Bihar, and Assam.

content.title: Key Institutional Steps
items: Collectivisation and consolidation of holdings.
Cooperation and abolition of Zamindari system.
Land reform was the main focus of the First Five Year Plan.

content.text: Government initiatives in the 1960s-70s included the Green Revolution (package technology) and White Revolution (Operation Flood).

content.title: Modern Financial & Technical Support
items: Kisan Credit Card (KCC) for easy credit.
Personal Accident Insurance Scheme (PAIS).
Crop insurance against drought, flood, fire, etc.
Minimum Support Price (MSP) to prevent exploitation by middlemen.
Special weather bulletins and agricultural programmes on Radio/TV.

content.text: Initiated by Vinoba Bhave (Gandhiji's spiritual heir). Shri Ram Chandra Reddy offered 80 acres of land to 80 landless villagers (Bhoodan). Some zamindars offered villages (Gramdan).

content.title: Significance
content.text: This movement aimed at voluntary land redistribution to provide livelihoods for the landless, avoiding the use of force or legal compulsion initially.

glossary.definition: A form of 'slash and burn' agriculture practised in north-eastern states where land is cleared and burnt for shifting cultivation.
Crops sown in winter (October-December) and harvested in summer (April-June).
Crops grown with the onset of monsoon and harvested in September-October.
A short cropping season during the summer months between rabi and kharif seasons.
A type of commercial farming where a single crop is grown on a large area using capital-intensive inputs.
The rearing of silkworms for the production of silk fibre.
A guaranteed price announced by the government to protect farmers from exploitation and ensure remunerative prices for their crops.
A movement initiated by Vinoba Bhave involving the voluntary donation of land by landowners to the landless.

summary_points: Agriculture is a primary economic activity in India, involving two-thirds of the population and providing food and industrial raw materials.
Farming types range from Primitive Subsistence (monsoon-dependent) and Intensive Subsistence (high pressure) to Commercial Farming (modern inputs).
India has three distinct cropping seasons: Rabi (winter), Kharif (monsoon), and Zaid (summer), ensuring year-round production of various crops.
Major food crops include rice, wheat, millets, and pulses, while non-food or commercial crops include tea, coffee, rubber, cotton, and jute.
The government has introduced various technological and institutional reforms (like Green Revolution, KCC, and MSP) to improve productivity and farmer welfare.
The Bhoodan-Gramdan movement by Vinoba Bhave stands as a unique 'Blood-less Revolution' for voluntary land redistribution in India.
